tex:
(a) Some people each recorded their favourite holiday destination.
\newline

    The results are shown in the pie chart.
\newline

    (i)  Complete the statements about the pie chart.
\newline

        The sector angle for Mexico is ... degrees.
\newline

        The most popular destination is ...

        $\dfrac{1}{5}$ of the people chose ...

        Three times as many people chose ... as ...

         180 people chose Bali.
\newline

        Find how many people were asked altogether.
\newline

        

(b) Mr and Mrs Baker go on holiday with their three children.
\newline

    They fly from Miami to Mexico City.
\newline

    (i)  The cost of each adult ticket is \$450.
\newline

        The cost of each child ticket is 70\% of the cost of an adult ticket.
\newline

        Calculate the total cost of the five tickets.
\newline

        

         The plane leaves Miami at 09$\,$29.
\newline

        It arrives in Mexico City 2 hours 11 minutes later.
\newline

        The local time in Miami is 1 hour ahead of the local time in Mexico City.
\newline

        Work out the time in Mexico City when the plane arrives.
\newline

        

         The family travels 38 kilometres by taxi.
\newline

        The journey costs \$3.50 plus an extra \$2.15 for each kilometre travelled.
\newline

        Find the cost of the journey.
\newline

        

         At the end of the holiday Mr Baker changes 1335 pesos into dollars.
\newline

        The exchange rate is \$1 = 17.8 pesos.
\newline

        Find how many dollars Mr Baker receives.
\newline

content.content:
Complete the statements about the pie chart.
\newline

        The sector angle for Mexico is \dotfill degrees.
\newline

        The most popular destination is \dotfill

        $\dfrac{1}{5}$ of the people chose \dotfill

        Three times as many people chose \dotfill as \dotfill

180 people chose Bali.
\newline

        Find how many people were asked altogether.
\newline

Mr and Mrs Baker go on holiday with their three children.
\newline

    They fly from Miami to Mexico City.
\newline

The cost of each adult ticket is \$450.
\newline

        The cost of each child ticket is 70\% of the cost of an adult ticket.
\newline

        Calculate the total cost of the five tickets.
\newline

The plane leaves Miami at 09$\,$29.
\newline

        It arrives in Mexico City 2 hours 11 minutes later.
\newline

        The local time in Miami is 1 hour ahead of the local time in Mexico City.
\newline

        Work out the time in Mexico City when the plane arrives.
\newline

The family travels 38 kilometres by taxi.
\newline

        The journey costs \$3.50 plus an extra \$2.15 for each kilometre travelled.
\newline

        Find the cost of the journey.
\newline

At the end of the holiday Mr Baker changes 1335 pesos into dollars.
\newline

        The exchange rate is \$1 = 17.8 pesos.
\newline

        Find how many dollars Mr Baker receives.
\newline

tex:
Shapes $A$, $B$ and $C$ are shown on the $1\,\text{cm}^2$ grid.
\newline

(a) Shape $A$ is a special type of quadrilateral.
\newline

    Write down the mathematical name for shape $A$.
\newline

    

(b) Describe fully the **single** transformation that maps

    (i)  shape $A$ onto shape $B$,

        

         shape $A$ onto shape $C$.
\newline

        

(c) On the grid,

    (i)  translate shape $A$ by the vector $\begin{pmatrix} 8 \\ -4 \end{pmatrix}$,

         reflect shape $A$ in the line $x = 2$.
\newline

(d) Find the area of **shape $B$**.
\newline

content.content:
Shape $A$ is a special type of quadrilateral.
\newline

    Write down the mathematical name for shape $A$.
\newline
Describe fully the \textbf{single} transformation that maps
shape $A$ onto shape $B$,

shape $A$ onto shape $C$.
\newline
On the grid,
translate shape $A$ by the vector $\begin{pmatrix} 8 \\ -4 \end{pmatrix}$,

reflect shape $A$ in the line $x = 2$.
\newline

Find the area of \textbf{shape $B$}.
\newline

tex:
The scale drawing shows the positions of three towns $A$, $B$ and $C$ on a map.
\newline

The scale is 1 centimetre represents 10 kilometres.
\newline

(a) Work out the actual distance between town $A$ and town $B$.
\newline

    

(b) (i)  Measure the bearing of town $C$ from town $A$.
\newline

        

         Show how to use your answer to **part (b)(i)** to find the bearing of town $A$ from town $C$.
\newline

        

(c) Town $D$ is $96\,\text{km}$ from town $C$ on a bearing of $100°$.
\newline

    (i)  Mark the position of town $D$ on the map.
\newline

         Jez drives from town $C$ to town $D$ in $1\dfrac{1}{2}$ hours.
\newline

        Work out his average speed.
\newline

        

         Change $96\,\text{km}$ into miles.
\newline

        Assume that $8\,\text{km}$ equals 5 miles.
\newline

content.content:
Work out the actual distance between town $A$ and town $B$.
\newline

Measure the bearing of town $C$ from town $A$.
\newline

Show how to use your answer to \textbf{part (b)(i)} to find the bearing of town $A$ from town $C$.
\newline

Town $D$ is $96\,\text{km}$ from town $C$ on a bearing of $100°$.
\newline

Mark the position of town $D$ on the map.
\newline

Jez drives from town $C$ to town $D$ in $1\dfrac{1}{2}$ hours.
\newline

        Work out his average speed.
\newline

Change $96\,\text{km}$ into miles.
\newline

        Assume that $8\,\text{km}$ equals 5 miles.
\newline

qtype: finding terms and rules for sequences from patterns
tex:
(a) The diagram shows the first three patterns in a sequence.
\newline

    On the grid, draw pattern 4.
\newline

(b) These are the first four terms of another sequence.
\newline
    \[41 \qquad 35 \qquad 29 \qquad 23\]

    (i)  Write down the next two terms.
\newline

        

         Write down the rule for continuing this sequence.
\newline

        

(c) These are the first four terms of a different sequence.
\newline
    \[11 \qquad 15 \qquad 19 \qquad 23\]

    (i)  Write down an expression for the $n$th term.
\newline

        

         Is 129 a term in this sequence?

        Show how you decide.
\newline

content.content:
These are the first four terms of another sequence.
\newline
    \[41 \qquad 35 \qquad 29 \qquad 23\]

Write down the next two terms.
\newline

Write down the rule for continuing this sequence.
\newline

These are the first four terms of a different sequence.
\newline
    \[11 \qquad 15 \qquad 19 \qquad 23\]

Write down an expression for the $n$th term.
\newline

Is 129 a term in this sequence?

        Show how you decide.
\newline

tex:
(a) Stef buys 3.5 kilograms of bananas.
\newline

    (i)  Bananas cost \$1.24 per kilogram.
\newline

        Stef pays with a \$5 note.
\newline

        Work out how much change she receives.
\newline

        

         Write 3.5 kilograms in grams.
\newline

        

(b) Oranges cost 85 cents each.
\newline

    Leo has a \$10 note.
\newline

    Work out the maximum number of oranges he can buy.
\newline

    

(c) 87\% of the mass of a pineapple is water.
\newline

    A pineapple has a mass of $700\,\text{g}$.
\newline

    Work out the mass of water in this pineapple.
\newline

    

(d) The number of melons sold in a shop each day for 7 days is shown below.
\newline
    \[18 \qquad 5 \qquad 23 \qquad 40 \qquad 28 \qquad 19 \qquad 17\]

    Work out the mean number of melons sold.
\newline

    

(e) Rio and Chi go to a fruit shop.
\newline

    Rio buys 4 apples and 2 plums for \$1.96.
\newline

    Chi buys 7 apples and 3 plums for \$3.24.
\newline

    Write down a pair of simultaneous equations and solve them to find the cost of 1 apple and the cost of 1 plum.
\newline

    You must show all your working.
\newline

content.content:
Stef buys 3.5 kilograms of bananas.
\newline

Bananas cost \$1.24 per kilogram.
\newline

        Stef pays with a \$5 note.
\newline

        Work out how much change she receives.
\newline

Write 3.5 kilograms in grams.
\newline

Oranges cost 85 cents each.
\newline

    Leo has a \$10 note.
\newline

    Work out the maximum number of oranges he can buy.
\newline

87\% of the mass of a pineapple is water.
\newline

    A pineapple has a mass of $700\,\text{g}$.
\newline

    Work out the mass of water in this pineapple.
\newline

The number of melons sold in a shop each day for 7 days is shown below.
\newline
    \[18 \qquad 5 \qquad 23 \qquad 40 \qquad 28 \qquad 19 \qquad 17\]

    Work out the mean number of melons sold.
\newline

Rio and Chi go to a fruit shop.
\newline

    Rio buys 4 apples and 2 plums for \$1.96.
\newline

    Chi buys 7 apples and 3 plums for \$3.24.
\newline

    Write down a pair of simultaneous equations and solve them to find the cost of 1 apple and the cost of 1 plum.
\newline

    You must show all your working.
\newline

_rawBody:
\begin{parts}
    \item Stef buys 3.5 kilograms of bananas.

    \begin{subparts}
        \subpart Bananas cost \$1.24 per kilogram.

        Stef pays with a \$5 note.

        Work out how much change she receives.
        \AnswerLine[\$][]
        \Marks{2}

        \subpart Write 3.5 kilograms in grams.
        \AnswerLine[][g]
        \Marks{1}
    \end{subparts}

    \item Oranges cost 85 cents each.

    Leo has a \$10 note.

    Work out the maximum number of oranges he can buy.
    \AnswerLine[][]
    \Marks{2}

    \item 87\% of the mass of a pineapple is water.

    A pineapple has a mass of 700\,g.

    Work out the mass of water in this pineapple.
    \AnswerLine[][g]
    \Marks{2}

    \item The number of melons sold in a shop each day for 7 days is shown below.
    \[18 \qquad 5 \qquad 23 \qquad 40 \qquad 28 \qquad 19 \qquad 17\]

    Work out the mean number of melons sold.
    \AnswerLine[][]
    \Marks{2}

    \item Rio and Chi go to a fruit shop.

    Rio buys 4 apples and 2 plums for \$1.96\,.

    Chi buys 7 apples and 3 plums for \$3.24\,.

    Write down a pair of simultaneous equations and solve them to find the cost of 1 apple and the cost of 1 plum.

    You must show all your working.
    \AnswerLine[Apple \$][]
    \AnswerLine[Plum \$][]
    \Marks{6}
\end{parts}

qtype: number representation, multiples, powers, roots, indices, HCF, LCM, prime factorization
tex:
(a) Write the number $602\,047$ in words.
\newline

    

(b) Find

    (i)  a multiple of 14,

        

         $56^2$,

        

         $\sqrt[3]{103\,823}$,

        

         $12^0$.
\newline

        

(c) Find the lowest common multiple (LCM) of 12 and 78.
\newline

    

(d) Find the highest common factor (HCF) of 12 and 78.
\newline

    

(e) Write 432 as a product of its prime factors.
\newline

content.content:
Write the number $602\,047$ in words.
\newline
Find
a multiple of 14,

$12^0$.
\newline

Find the lowest common multiple (LCM) of 12 and 78.
\newline

Find the highest common factor (HCF) of 12 and 78.
\newline

Write 432 as a product of its prime factors.
\newline

tex:
(a) Complete the table of values for $y = \dfrac{6}{x}$.
\newline

    \begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
    \hline
    $x$ & $-6$ & $-5$ & $-4$ & $-3$ & $-2$ & $-1$ & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 & 6 \\
    \hline
    $y$ & $-1$ & & & $-2$ & $-3$ & $-6$ & 6 & 3 & 2 & & 1.2 & 1 \\
    \hline
    \end{tabular}

(b) On the grid, draw the graph of $y = \dfrac{6}{x}$ for $-6 \leqslant x \leqslant -1$ and $1 \leqslant x \leqslant 6$.
\newline

    

(c) Use your graph to solve the equation $\dfrac{6}{x} = 4.5$.
\newline

    

(d) (i)  On the grid, draw the line $y = x$.
\newline

         Write down the co-ordinates of the points of intersection of $y = \dfrac{6}{x}$ and $y = x$.
\newline

content.content:
Complete the table of values for $y = \dfrac{6}{x}$.
\newline

Use your graph to solve the equation $\dfrac{6}{x} = 4.5$.
\newline

On the grid, draw the line $y = x$.
\newline

Write down the co-ordinates of the points of intersection of $y = \dfrac{6}{x}$ and $y = x$.
\newline

tex:
(a) A bag contains 20 bulbs.
\newline

    8 are yellow, 5 are red, 4 are white and 3 are pink.
\newline

    Sam takes one bulb at random.
\newline

    Find the probability that the bulb he takes is

    (i)  white,

        

         blue,

        

         not pink.
\newline

        

(b) Sam has a rectangular pond, $ABCD$.
\newline

    (i)  Calculate $BC$.
\newline

        

         He puts a fence around the edge of the pond.
\newline

        Calculate the length of the fence.
\newline

        

(c) A scale drawing of Sam's garden, $PQRS$, is shown below.
\newline

    The scale is 1 centimetre represents 4 metres.
\newline

    Sam plants some bulbs so that they are
    (d) less than 30 metres from $P$
    and
    (e) nearer to $PQ$ than to $PS$.
\newline
    **Using a ruler and compasses only**, construct and shade the region where he plants the bulbs.
\newline

content.content:
A bag contains 20 bulbs.
\newline

    8 are yellow, 5 are red, 4 are white and 3 are pink.
\newline

    Sam takes one bulb at random.
\newline

    Find the probability that the bulb he takes is
white,
blue,

not pink.
\newline

Calculate $BC$.
\newline

He puts a fence around the edge of the pond.
\newline

        Calculate the length of the fence.
\newline

nearer to $PQ$ than to $PS$.
\newline
    \end{itemize}

    \textbf{Using a ruler and compasses only}, construct and shade the region where he plants the bulbs.
\newline

tex:
$A$, $B$ and $C$ are points on the circumference of the circle, centre $O$.
\newline

The straight line $DE$ touches the circle at $B$.
\newline

(a) Write down the mathematical name for the line $DE$.
\newline

    

(b) On the circle, draw a radius.
\newline

(c) Complete the following statements.
\newline

    (i)  Angle $ABD$ = ... because ...

        

         Angle $ACB$ = ... because ...

        

(d) $AB = 9\,\text{cm}$.
\newline

    (i)  Calculate the area of the circle.
\newline

        Give the units of your answer.
\newline

        

         Calculate $BC$.
\newline

content.content:
Write down the mathematical name for the line $DE$.
\newline

On the circle, draw a radius.
\newline

Complete the following statements.
\newline
Angle $ABD$ = \dotfill because \dotfill
Angle $ACB$ = \dotfill because \dotfill

$AB = 9\,\text{cm}$.
\newline

Calculate the area of the circle.
\newline

        Give the units of your answer.
\newline

Calculate $BC$.
\newline
